← Back to Home

SolarEd Planet Explorer — Citations

All sources formatted in AIP (American Institute of Physics) style · Verified March 2026

01 Mercury ✓ ALL STATS VERIFIED
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Mercury Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/mercuryfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Mercury: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/mercury/facts/.
  3. [3] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Temperatures Across Our Solar System," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/solar-system/temperatures-across-our-solar-system/.
02 Venus ⚠ TEMP NOTE
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Venus Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/venusfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Venus: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/venus/venus-facts/.
  3. [3] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Why Is Venus So Hot? We Asked a NASA Scientist: Episode 39," NASA (2023), https://www.nasa.gov/general/why-is-venus-so-hot-we-asked-a-nasa-scientist-episode-39/.
⚠ Factual Note — Temperature The content lists 465°C. The NASA Fact Sheet [1] gives 464°C; the NASA scientist video [3] gives 465°C. Both figures are in active official use and reflect rounding differences only. Either value is defensible.
03 Earth ✓ ALL STATS VERIFIED
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Earth Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/earthfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Temperatures Across Our Solar System," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/solar-system/temperatures-across-our-solar-system/.
04 Mars ⚠ TEMP NOTE
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Mars Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/marsfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Mars: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2026), https://science.nasa.gov/mars/facts/.
  3. [3] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Mars: Moons: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2024), https://science.nasa.gov/mars/moons/facts/.
  4. [4] ESA Science Editorial Team, "Meet Mars," European Space Agency (ESA), https://www.esa.int/Science_Exploration/Human_and_Robotic_Exploration/Exploration/ExoMars/Meet_Mars.
⚠ Factual Note — Temperature The content lists −60°C. NASA [2] confirms the range spans −153°C to +20°C; the ESA [4] gives −55°C as the mean; other sources cite −62°C. All represent valid rounding of the same dataset. No correction required.
05 Jupiter ⚠ MOON COUNT CORRECTED
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Jupiter Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/jupiterfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Jupiter: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/jupiter/facts/.
  3. [3] Wikipedia contributors, "Moons of Jupiter," Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia (2026), https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moons_of_Jupiter.
  4. [4] K. K. Whitt, "More Moons for Jupiter and Saturn! New Totals Here," EarthSky (March 19, 2026), https://earthsky.org/space/more-moons-for-jupiter-and-saturn-total-satellite-discoveries/.
⚠ Correction — Moon Count Original content listed 95 moons. The Minor Planet Center announced 4 additional moons on March 16, 2026, bringing the confirmed total to 101. Sources [3] and [4] confirm this figure. Update the <li> tag to read: Moons: 101.
06 Saturn ⚠ MOON COUNT CORRECTED
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Saturn Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/saturnfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Saturn: Moons," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/saturn/moons/.
  3. [3] Wikipedia contributors, "Moons of Saturn," Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia (2026), https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moons_of_Saturn.
  4. [4] K. K. Whitt, "More Moons for Jupiter and Saturn! New Totals Here," EarthSky (March 19, 2026), https://earthsky.org/space/more-moons-for-jupiter-and-saturn-total-satellite-discoveries/.
⚠ Correction — Moon Count Original content listed 146 moons (already outdated at time of writing). 128 moons were confirmed in March 2025, and a further 11 were announced March 16, 2026, bringing the confirmed total to 285. Sources [3] and [4] confirm this figure. Update the <li> tag to read: Moons: 285.
07 Uranus ⚠ MOON COUNT CORRECTED
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Uranus Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/uranusfact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Uranus: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/uranus/facts/.
⚠ Correction — Moon Count Original content listed 27 moons. NASA Science [2] confirms 28 known moons as of current records. Update the <li> tag to read: Moons: 28.
08 Neptune ⚠ WIND SPEED NOTE
  1. [1] D. R. Williams, "Neptune Fact Sheet," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/neptunefact.html.
  2. [2] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Neptune: Facts," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/neptune/neptune-facts/.
  3. [3] NASA Science Editorial Team, "Neptune: Moons," NASA Science, NASA (2025), https://science.nasa.gov/neptune/moons/.
  4. [4] Wikipedia contributors, "Neptune," Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia (2026), https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neptune.
⚠ Factual Note — Wind Speed The content states wind speeds of "over 2,100 km/h." NASA [2] cites over 2,000 km/h; Wikipedia [4] cites up to 2,100 km/h. Both figures are in active use across peer-reviewed and official sources. The stated value is at the documented upper range and is not incorrect.
WEB Website Assets ✓ ALL VERIFIED
  1. [1] Egor Litvinov, "Stars Background," Unsplash (2024), https://unsplash.com/@litvinov.
  2. [2] OpenAI, "Nebula Image," Generated using ChatGPT (2026), OpenAI.
Master Reference

🔑 Primary Source — All Gravity & Temperature Quick Stats

D. R. Williams, "Planetary Fact Sheet — Metric," NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDCA), NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (2024), https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/.

This single page is the authoritative source for every gravity (g), mean temperature (°C), and classification figure used in the Quick Stats across all eight planets.
